Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First we need to count the number of events per case id We can do this by grouping by case id and counting the number of rows per group

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then we want to filter out any case id that has more than six events This gives us a list of case ids that have 6 or fewer events

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Finally we join this filtered list of case ids back to the original event log table to get all the events for those cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: We can use a common table expression CTE or a subquery

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Approach using a subquery in the WHERE clause

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But note This might be inefficient for large tables because the subquery is executed for each row Actually the subquery is executed once and then the IN condition is checked

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Alternatively we can use a CTE or a derived table and then join However the above should work

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Another approach is to use a window function to assign a count per case and then filter

Name: Validation - IND
Text: This approach adds a column event count per case and then filters It might be less efficient in terms of memory because it adds a column and the entire table is processed

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: However note that the requirement is to return only the events all columns for the cases that have six or fewer events

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Both methods are valid We will choose the window function method because it is straightforward

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: But note the window function method returns an extra column The problem says return only the events meaning all the original columns We do not want the extra column

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: DuckDB supports the EXCLUDE clause in the SELECT list

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Option 1 List all the original columns which we do not know exactly not feasible Option 2 Use a CTE to first compute the counts and then join back to the original table without the extra column

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Let me propose a CTE approach WITH case counts AS SELECT case id COUNT as event count FROM event log GROUP BY case id SELECT el FROM event log el JOIN case counts cc ON el case id equals cc case id WHERE cc event count is less than or equal to 6

Name: Validation - IND
Text: This is also a common pattern and avoids the extra column

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Let me choose the CTE method with a join because it is standard SQL and does not require EXCLUDE

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Alternatively we can use a subquery in the WHERE clause without CTE SELECT FROM event log WHERE case id IN SELECT case id FROM event log GROUP BY case id HAVING COUNT less than or equal to 6

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets test with a small example Suppose case 1 has 3 events included and case 2 has 7 events excluded Then the subquery returns case id 1 and the main query returns all events of case id 1 This is correct

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The subquery is a non correlated subquery It is executed once

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: We will go with the subquery method for simplicity

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The subquery groups by case id and then filters the groups Then the outer query uses that to filter the rows

Name: Validation - IND
Text: What if there are NULL case ids The above query will handle that
